text:
In 1921, two brothers from Guangdong arrived in Bangkok and opened a seed shop in Yaowarat, the city's Chinatown district. They had no local connections, no outside capital, and no guarantee of survival. One hundred years later, their descendants control a conglomerate with operations in 21 countries, roughly 14,000 convenience stores across Thailand, and the country's largest mobile network.

That company is CP Group, short for Charoen Pokphand, which translates roughly as "progressive agriculture." Most people outside Southeast Asia have never heard of it. That obscurity is not an accident.

The founding generation sold seeds. The second generation, led by Dhanin Chearavanont, understood that seeds were just the entry point. You could sell the seeds to the farmer, then sell the farmer the feed for his livestock, then buy back the animals for processing, then sell the packaged product to the retailer. The margin was not in any single step. It was in controlling every step.

That logic became CP Foods, which by the early 2020s was producing over three million tons of chicken annually, supplying quick-service restaurant chains across Asia. The company's name appears on no consumer label in those supply chains. You have very likely eaten CP Group's protein without knowing it.

In 1978, when China began opening to foreign investment, one of the first companies the government invited in was CP Group. Dhanin Chearavanont arrived in Shenzhen before the special economic zone had roads. According to CP Group's corporate history and contemporaneous accounts from the period, the company received one of the first foreign investment certificates issued in China under the reform framework. By the early 1990s, it was operating as China's largest foreign investor by deployed capital.

That China relationship brought both scale and political complexity. CP Group's ties to the People's Republic made it simultaneously the most globally connected and the most carefully positioned conglomerate in Thailand. Its senior executives do not give many press interviews.

The Thailand-facing play was just as deliberate. In 1989, CP Group's subsidiary CP All secured the exclusive franchise to operate 7-Eleven convenience stores in Thailand. At the time, the network was small. What followed was one of the most consistent retail buildouts in Southeast Asian commercial history.

edited_script.title: CP Group Part 1: Seed Shop to Invisible Empire
edited_script.description: Origins of CP Group from 1921 Bangkok seed shop through China FDI and agri-food vertical integration.

narration.text: In 1921, two brothers from Guangdong arrived in Bangkok and opened a seed shop in Yaowarat, the city's Chinatown district. They had no local connections, no outside capital, and no guarantee of survival. One hundred years later, their descendants control a conglomerate with operations in 21 countries, roughly 14,000 convenience stores across Thailand, and the country's largest mobile network.

visuals.prompt: Cinematic establishing shot of a narrow market laneway in Bangkok Chinatown district in the 1920s era. Wooden shophouses line both sides. Chinese calligraphy painted on signboards above open doorways. Seed sacks and small clay pots arranged on a low wooden platform outside one shop front. Warm amber dawn light filtering through the lane. Street-level perspective, slightly wide angle. No people. No human silhouettes. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.

scenes.title: CP Group: The Name Nobody Knows

narration.text: That company is CP Group, short for Charoen Pokphand, which translates roughly as "progressive agriculture." Most people outside Southeast Asia have never heard of it. That obscurity is not an accident.

visuals.prompt: Infographic editorial illustration: Southeast Asia regional map with Thailand prominently highlighted in deep corporate blue. Network lines radiating outward toward labeled country markers across Asia, Europe, and the Americas. Clean white background. Minimalist data-visualization style with thin line-work. No people, no human figures. NEGATIVE: no seeds, no food items, no agricultural products, no convenience store imagery. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.

visuals.prompt: Isometric business infographic diagram illustrating a four-stage vertical supply chain. Top tier: grain silo. Second tier: animal feed mill with auger conveyor. Third tier: large-scale farm complex aerial view. Bottom tier: retail supermarket shelf. Each stage connected by bold downward arrows in corporate gold. Deep blue and gold color palette on white background. Clean data-visualization style, not photorealistic. No people, no human figures. NEGATIVE: no seed packets or seeds visible, no restaurant logos, no fast-food imagery, no CP branding. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.

scenes.title: CP Foods: Invisible Protein Empire
text_overlays: No brand label on supply chain

visuals.prompt: Aerial cinematic shot of a large-scale industrial food processing complex. Vast rectangular processing buildings surrounded by flat agricultural land. Steam rising from ventilation stacks. Corporate documentary photography style. Muted earth tones and grey industrial palette. No identifiable brand signage. No faces, no workers visible. NEGATIVE: no seeds, no seed shop, no Yaowarat laneway setting, no restaurant branding, no fast-food logos. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. visuals.prompt: Cinematic archival-style photograph of a vast flat construction site in southern China circa late 1970s. Red clay earth, a single unpaved access road stretching to the horizon, construction cranes barely visible in the far distance. Faded documentary film color palette, slightly desaturated warm tones suggesting analog photography. No people visible. NEGATIVE: no food items, no chicken, no seeds, no agricultural products, no convenience stores, no modern city skyline. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.

visuals.prompt: Macro close-up of a vintage official government investment certificate document. Heavy bond paper with faint watermark texture. Bold red institutional chop stamp in the lower corner. Traditional bureaucratic typography. Official border with red-and-gold design elements. Dramatic side-raking directional light revealing paper grain. Shallow depth of field, background softly dark. No faces, no people. NEGATIVE: no food, no agricultural products, no convenience stores, no modern office. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.

scenes.title: Political Complexity: Two Capitals, One Structure

visuals.prompt: Abstract editorial graphic: two large geometric color-field panels, one warm red-and-gold, one cool red-white-and-blue, partially overlapping at a precise diagonal center line against a neutral charcoal grey background. The overlapping intersection rendered in a neutral tone. Clean geometric graphic design aesthetic. No national emblems or literal flag elements. No people, no human figures, no faces. NEGATIVE: no food items, no agricultural products, no seeds, no convenience stores, no skyline or buildings. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.

visuals.prompt: Cinematic night exterior shot of a 7-Eleven convenience store on a Thai urban street. Bright green and red neon signage casts warm colored light onto wet pavement. Power lines visible overhead against a deep blue-black sky. Street-level establishing shot from across the lane. Warm inviting ambient glow from the store interior. No people, no human silhouettes. NEGATIVE: no seeds, no agricultural products, no poultry imagery, no processing facility, no maps or diagrams. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.
